disease.name: Postoperative Ileus
disease.note: Temporary impairment of gastrointestinal motility after surgery. Common after abdominal surgery. Differentiate from mechanical bowel obstruction. Typically self-limiting but can be prolonged (paralytic ileus lasting > 3-5 days should prompt investigation for underlying cause). In Kenyan settings where ERAS protocols are not routinely implemented, prolonged ileus from opioid use and delayed mobilization is common. Always check electrolytes before labeling it simple ileus.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: If peritonitis/feculent vomiting/hypotension -> SEVERE (suspect mechanical obstruction or complication); else -> NON-SEVERE (paralytic ileus)

history_questions.question: Did your bowels work normally before the operation?
Have you passed any gas since surgery?
Have you opened your bowels since surgery?
Is your belly getting bigger?
Have you vomited?
Is the vomit green or yellow? Or like stool?
Is the pain colicky (cramping) or constant?
Is the pain getting worse?
Are you on strong painkillers?
When did you last eat or drink?
Have you been getting out of bed?
Do you have a fever?
Were you opened up before for the same issue?
Do you feel bloated or just uncomfortable?
Are you thirsty?
What type of surgery did you have?
Was the surgery an emergency or planned?
Have you had problems with constipation before?
Do you have diabetes or thyroid problems?
Was your pain colicky (wave-like, coming and going) or constant and dull?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries?
Have you had abdominal surgery or injuries in the past that required a hospital stay?
When did you last pass gas, and have you passed any since?
Are you taking any pain medication, and what kind (especially morphine or pethidine)?
Do you have any sensation of needing to pass stool but being unable to?
Is the distension worse in the morning or evening, or is it constant?
Did you have similar problems with bowel function before the surgery?
What is your normal bowel habit (daily, every other day, etc.)?
Do you take any medications for constipation at home?
Have you been walking or getting out of bed after your surgery?
Did you receive an enema or bowel preparation before surgery?
Have you had any coffee or tea since surgery?
Do you feel bloated all the time or only after eating or drinking?
Do you have any history of diabetes, hypothyroidism, or other endocrine conditions?
Are you on any medications for blood pressure, depression, or parkinsonism?
What color is the fluid coming out of your nasogastric tube (if present)?
Have you had any episodes of vomiting -- is it food, green fluid, or dark fluid?
Have you passed any gas or stool since your surgery? When?
Do you have any abdominal pain? Is it constant or cramping?
Are you feeling nauseated? Does it improve after vomiting?
Have you been out of bed since surgery?
What pain medications are you receiving? How often?
Do you have a fever or chills?
Is your abdomen getting bigger (do your clothes feel tighter)?
Have you had previous abdominal surgeries? How many?
Do you have a history of adhesions or small bowel obstructions?
Have you had any hernia repairs with mesh?
Do you have inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's, ulcerative colitis)?
Have you had radiation therapy to your abdomen or pelvis?
Are you able to keep down small sips of water?

examination_findings.finding: Abdominal distension
Absent or reduced bowel sounds
Tympanic percussion note (drum-like)
Mild diffuse abdominal tenderness
No guarding or rigidity
High NG tube output (>1 L/day)
Scaphoid abdomen (if NG decompressed)
Dehydration signs (dry mucosa, reduced skin turgor)
Tachycardia (if hypovolemic)
Hypotension (if dehydrated)
Electrolyte disturbance signs (muscle weakness, confusion)
Visible peristalsis (rare in ileus, suggests obstruction)
Abdominal scar from previous surgery
Colicky pain on auscultation with high-pitched tinkling bowel sounds (suggests early mechanical obstruction)
Borborygmi (loud, prolonged gurgling sounds -- suggests resolving ileus or early obstruction)
Shifting dullness on percussion (presence of ascites or intra-abdominal fluid)
Psoas sign or obturator sign (suggests retrocecal abscess as cause of ileus)
Visible peristalsis on abdominal inspection (more common in thin patients with obstruction)
Abdominal wall edema (fluid overload contributing to bowel wall edema)
Cecostomy or stoma site: absent output suggests obstruction, high output suggests ileus or short bowel
Fluid thrill on abdominal examination (ascites from underlying pathology)
Scaphoid abdomen after NG decompression (effective decompression -- positive sign)
Metabolic alkalosis from prolonged NG suction (hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis)
Hypokalemia on ECG (flattened T waves, U waves, prolonged QT -- suggests electrolyte cause of ileus)
Hypoactive bowel sounds in all four quadrants with irregular pattern
Dehydration signs: dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes
Cecal diameter >12 cm on abdominal X-ray (Ogilvie syndrome -- acute colonic pseudo-obstruction)
Air-fluid levels on serial X-rays that are stationary or progressing (suggests obstruction not ileus)

historyFeatures.note: Pain is usually dull and diffuse, not colicky as in mechanical obstruction

examFeatures.note: Absence suggests ileus rather than peritonitis

bedside_scores.name: Ileus Severity Score (Clinical)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=5 suggests prolonged ileus requiring investigation for underlying cause.

subtypes.name: Physiologic (Post-Laparotomy) Ileus
subtypes.management: Conservative: NPO, NG tube if vomiting, IV fluids, electrolyte correction, early mobilization, minimize opioids. Gum chewing (sham feeding). Usually resolves in 2-4 days with return of bowel sounds and passage of flatus.

subtypes.name: Prolonged (Paralytic) Ileus
subtypes.management: Exclude mechanical obstruction (CT with oral contrast). Correct electrolytes aggressively (K+ >4.0, Mg++ >0.8, PO4 >1.0). Switch opioids to epidural analgesia or NSAIDs if possible. Consider prokinetics: Metoclopramide 10 mg TDS, Erythromycin 200 mg TDS IV. Parenteral nutrition if NPO >7 days.

subtypes.name: Ogilvie Syndrome (Acute Colonic Pseudo-obstruction)
subtypes.management: NPO, NG tube, rectal tube. Correct electrolytes. Neostigmine 2.0-2.5 mg IV over 3-5 min with cardiac monitoring. Atropine at bedside. If no response: colonoscopic decompression. If cecal diameter >12 cm or signs of ischemia: emergency laparotomy with cecostomy or resection.

discriminators: epigastric pain relieved by prone position
duodenal obstruction on upper GI series
history of scoliosis surgery or rapid weight loss
ruleOutTests: CT with oral contrast showing aortomesenteric angle <22 degrees

discriminators: distant source of infection (pneumonia, UTI, line sepsis)
no abdominal pathology found on imaging
ruleOutTests: CT abdomen to exclude primary abdominal cause

discriminators: NG output >500 mL/day with minimal abdominal distension
sensation of fullness after small meals
may have history of connective tissue disorder or amyloidosis

bedside.name: NG tube output measurement
bedside.indication: high output suggests ileus or obstruction

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray (Erect + Supine)
imaging.indication: differentiate ileus (generalized gas) from obstruction (air-fluid levels, dilated loops)

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with oral contrast
imaging.indication: rule out mechanical obstruction or abscess if prolonged ileus

management.protocolReference: ERAS (Enhanced Recovery After Surgery) Guidelines for Ileus Prevention

resuscitation: NG tube on free drainage if vomiting or distension severe
Urinary catheter if output monitoring needed
analgesia: Switch from opioids to multimodal analgesia
NSAIDs (Ibuprofen 400 mg TDS or Diclofenac 50 mg TDS) if no renal impairment
monitoring: Daily abdominal exam and girth measurement
Return of bowel sounds and flatus

simple_ileus.step5: Remove NG tube when output <500 mL/day
simple_ileus.step6: Start sips, then clear fluids, then soft diet as bowel sounds return

prolonged_ileus.description: Active intervention for ileus beyond day 4
prolonged_ileus.step1: CT abdomen to exclude mechanical obstruction
prolonged_ileus.step3: Consider prokinetics: Metoclopramide 10 mg TDS IV, Erythromycin 200 mg IV TDS
prolonged_ileus.step5: Parenteral nutrition if NPO >7 days anticipated

general: Early mobilization — out of bed day 1
diet_advancement: Day 1-2: Sips if bowel sounds return

pathways.criteria: post-laparotomy day 1-3, distension mild, no peritonitis, stable vitals
pathways.treatment: NPO, NG tube PRN for vomiting. IV crystalloids. Correct electrolytes. Minimize opioid use (epidural preferable). Early mobilization. Chewing gum (evidence for sham feeding). Remove NG tube when output < 500 mL/day.

pathways.criteria: no bowel function > 4 days, distension severe, failed conservative management
pathways.treatment: CT abdomen to exclude obstruction. Correct K+/Mg+/PO4. Consider prokinetics (Metoclopramide 10 mg TDS, Erythromycin 200 mg IV TDS). Switch to epidural analgesia. TPN if oral intake not anticipated for > 7 days.

operative_templates.procedure: Diagnostic Laparoscopy for Suspected Mechanical Obstruction
operative_templates.indication: Prolonged ileus with CT findings suspicious for mechanical obstruction
operative_templates.incision: 10 mm umbilical port, 5 mm right and left flank ports
steps: Insert camera and inspect abdominal cavity
Look for transition point, adhesions, or internal hernia
If adhesive band found: divide with scissors/energy device
If no mechanical cause found: abort, close ports
If bowel ischemia encountered: convert to open laparotomy

watchFor: transition from absent to colicky pain

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: After abdominal surgery, the bowels can be 'lazy' or 'sleepy' and stop moving temporarily. This is called postoperative ileus. It usually resolves on its own within a few days as your body recovers.
patient_education.warningSigns: Worsening abdominal pain, vomiting feculent material, high fever, inability to pass gas for more than 4-5 days after surgery.
patient_education.activity: Getting out of bed and walking is the best treatment. Walk several times a day as tolerated.
patient_education.diet: Start with sips of water when your surgeon says it is safe. Progress to soft foods as tolerated. Avoid gas-forming foods initially.
patient_education.medication: Pain medications (especially morphine-like drugs) can slow the bowels. Your doctors will try to minimize these.
patient_education.followUp: Return to clinic in 2 weeks for wound check and bowel function assessment.

before_discharge: Dietary counseling on gradual food advancement

after_discharge.action: Phone call or clinic review — bowel function check
Final follow-up — full diet tolerance, return to normal activities

when_to_return: Inability to eat or drink without vomiting
No bowel movement for 3 days after discharge

pathophysiologyDetail: Postoperative ileus (POI) represents a transient impairment of coordinated gastrointestinal motility following surgery, driven by a complex neuro-inflammatory cascade. The pathophysiology involves three integrated mechanisms: (1) neural inhibition via spinal afferent and efferent sympathetic pathways activated by peritoneal incision and bowel manipulation, which suppress enteric parasympathetic activity and abolish the migrating motor complex; (2) a robust inflammatory response mediated by resident macrophages and mast cells in the muscularis externa that release pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-alpha, IL-1beta) and nitric oxide, directly inhibiting smooth muscle contractility; and (3) pharmacologic suppression from opioid analgesics acting on mu-receptors in the enteric nervous system, which reduce acetylcholine release and increase non-propulsive segmental contractions.

The natural history of POI follows a predictable timeline. Physiologic ileus affects the stomach first (returning to function within 24-48 hours), followed by the small intestine (24-72 hours), and finally the colon (48-120 hours). This temporal gradient explains why a patient may be passing flatus but still have abdominal distension -- the colon is the last to recover. In Kenyan surgical populations, where ERAS (Enhanced Recovery After Surgery) protocols are not routinely implemented, POI is frequently prolonged by several factors: delayed mobilization due to cultural beliefs about postoperative rest, reliance on parenteral opioid analgesia (pethidine and morphine remain the most affordable parenteral analgesics), and uncorrected electrolyte abnormalities, particularly hypokalemia from inadequate perioperative potassium replacement. Prolonged POI lasting beyond postoperative day 4 should prompt investigation for an underlying pathologic cause -- most commonly an anastomotic leak, intra-abdominal abscess, or mechanical obstruction from adhesions or internal hernia.

The differential between POI and mechanical bowel obstruction is critical and frequently challenging. Key distinguishing features: POI presents with constant dull discomfort and diffuse abdominal distension with absent bowel sounds, whereas mechanical obstruction produces progressive colicky pain, hyperactive or high-pitched tinkling bowel sounds, and radiographic evidence of a transition point with air-fluid levels on erect abdominal X-ray. CT with oral contrast is definitive for identifying mechanical obstruction when the clinical picture is ambiguous. In Kenyan settings where CT is not immediately accessible, serial abdominal examinations and plain radiographic assessment of gas distribution patterns remain the mainstay of initial differentiation.

landmarkTrials.trial: ERAS (Enhanced Recovery After Surgery) Protocol for Ileus Reduction
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 21 RCTs (4,500 patients) comparing ERAS vs conventional care in colorectal surgery
landmarkTrials.intervention: Multimodal ERAS bundle: epidural analgesia, early enteral nutrition, early mobilization, opioid-sparing analgesia, fluid restriction
landmarkTrials.outcome: Reduced time to return of bowel function by 24-36 hours, reduced hospital stay by 2.5 days, reduced POI rate by 40% (OR 0.60)
landmarkTrials.impact: ERAS became standard of care; demonstrates that POI is highly modifiable through non-pharmacologic interventions
landmarkTrials.reference: Greco M, et al. Enhanced recovery after surgery for colorectal surgery: a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. World J Surg. 2016;40(5):1213-1224.

landmarkTrials.trial: Gum Chewing for Ileus Prevention Meta-analysis
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 17 RCTs (1,837 patients) undergoing abdominal surgery
landmarkTrials.intervention: Chewing gum (sham feeding) 3-4 times daily starting immediately postoperatively
landmarkTrials.outcome: Reduced time to first flatus by 10.4 hours, first bowel movement by 15.2 hours, and hospital stay by 1.8 days
landmarkTrials.impact: Low-cost, zero-risk intervention ideal for resource-limited settings. Widely implemented in Kenyan surgical wards.
landmarkTrials.reference: Yang Y, et al. Gum chewing for promoting gastrointestinal recovery after abdominal surgery. Gastroenterol Res Pract. 2015;2015:598649.

landmarkTrials.trial: PROVIDE (IV Lidocaine for Postoperative Ileus Prevention)
landmarkTrials.population: RCT of 210 patients undergoing elective colorectal surgery
landmarkTrials.intervention: IV lidocaine (1.5 mg/kg bolus + 2 mg/kg/hr infusion for 24 hours) vs placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: Reduced time to first flatus by 12 hours (p=0.02), reduced opioid consumption by 35%, and reduced hospital stay by 1.5 days
landmarkTrials.impact: IV lidocaine provides both analgesic and prokinetic benefits; alternative to epidural when resources limited
landmarkTrials.reference: Ventham NT, et al. Systemic lidocaine for postoperative ileus in colorectal surgery. Br J Surg. 2017;104(8):1006-1015.

landmarkTrials.population: 1,260 patients undergoing partial bowel resection with primary anastomosis across 50 US centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Alvimopan 12 mg PO preoperatively and twice daily postoperatively vs placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: Reduced time to GI recovery (GI-2 endpoint) by 12-18 hours; reduced hospital stay by 1 day (p<0.001)
landmarkTrials.impact: Alvimopan is FDA-approved for POI prevention but high cost limits use in Kenya; demonstrates opioid-receptor antagonism as valid therapeutic target
landmarkTrials.reference: Ludwig K, et al. Alvimopan for the management of postoperative ileus after bowel resection. Am J Surg. 2008;195(5):617-623.

evidenceTable.intervention: Structured mobilization protocol (out of bed first postoperative day)
evidenceTable.outcome: POI decreased from 28% to 16%; hospital stay -2.1 days

evidenceTable.intervention: Lidocaine 1.5 mg/kg bolus + 2 mg/min infusion for 24h
evidenceTable.outcome: Time to flatus -14h; opioid consumption -30%; LOS -1.5 days
Time to first flatus -15h; time to first bowel movement -18h

evidenceTable.study: Erythromycin as Prokinetic After Abdominal Surgery (2015)
evidenceTable.intervention: Erythromycin 200 mg IV q8h vs placebo
evidenceTable.outcome: Reduced time to flatus (-8h) and bowel movement (-12h); no effect on LOS
Clinical decompression in 80-90%; 10% recurrence requiring repeat dose

evidenceTable.study: Epidural vs IV Opioid Analgesia and Ileus (2017)
evidenceTable.intervention: Thoracic epidural (bupivacaine + opioid) vs IV opioid PCA
evidenceTable.outcome: Epidural: POI reduced 40%; time to GI recovery -22h; LOS -1.5 days

evidenceTable.study: Postoperative GI Recovery in Kenyan Laparotomy Patients (2021)
evidenceTable.outcome: Mean time to flatus 4.2 days; prolonged ileus in 22%; predictors: opioid dose, hypokalemia, delayed mobilization

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: DIFFERENTIATE RESOLVING VS PROLONGED VS COMPLICATED ILEUS. Step 1 -- TIMING: Resolving physiologic ileus: postoperative days 1-3, progressive improvement, passage of flatus by day 3, bowel sounds returning. Prolonged ileus: no passage of flatus or stool by day 4, persistent distension, high NG output. Complicated ileus: associated tachycardia, fever, rising CRP, worsening pain, peritonism. Step 2 -- EXAMINATION: Auscultate bowel sounds (absent vs tinkling vs high-pitched obstructive), percuss for tympany, palpate for tenderness (diffuse mild vs focal vs peritonism). Measure abdominal girth serially. Check for scars suggesting previous surgery with possible adhesive obstruction. Step 3 -- LABORATORY: Check electrolytes (K+, Mg++, PO4, Ca++), serum creatinine, and CRP. Rising CRP with tachycardia mandates CT to exclude anastomotic leak or abscess. Step 4 -- IMAGING: Erect and supine abdominal X-ray: generalized gas throughout the bowel without air-fluid levels suggests ileus; multiple air-fluid levels with dilated loops and step-ladder pattern suggests mechanical obstruction. CT abdomen with oral contrast (the definitive test): if transition point present, diagnosis is mechanical obstruction; if diffuse dilatation without transition point, consistent with ileus. Step 5 -- MANAGEMENT: For physiologic ileus: NPO, NG tube if distension severe, correct electrolytes, minimize opioids, early mobilization, gum chewing. For prolonged ileus without mechanical cause: add prokinetics (Metoclopramide 10 mg TDS IV, Erythromycin 200 mg TDS IV), switch to epidural analgesia if available, trial of neostigmine (2.0-2.5 mg IV slow push) for colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie syndrome). For mechanical obstruction: surgical consult, exploratory laparoscopy or laparotomy. The single most important decision point is to distinguish simple prolonged ileus from mechanical obstruction -- CT with contrast is the definitive arbiter and should not be delayed beyond postoperative day 4-5 in the absence of bowel function.

managementPearls.pearl: The three most common reversible causes of prolonged ileus are hypokalemia (target K+ >4.0 mmol/L), opioid excess, and missed mechanical obstruction. Address all three before labeling it 'refractory ileus.'

Chewing gum three times daily is the most cost-effective intervention for POI prevention in resource-limited settings -- it provides sham feeding that stimulates cephalic-vagal reflexes, increases salivary and pancreatic secretions, and releases GI hormones (gastrin, neurotensin) that promote motility. Cost: essentially zero.

Switch from morphine to NSAIDs (ketorolac, ibuprofen, diclofenac) or tramadol as soon as pain permits. Morphine delays gastric emptying by 40-60% and prolongs colonic transit by 50%. NSAIDs have the added benefit of reducing the inflammatory component of POI through COX-2 inhibition.

Coffee enemas: One small RCT showed a 15-hour reduction in time to first flatus compared to placebo in post-cesarean ileus. While not standard of care, it is a low-risk, low-cost option that may benefit selected patients in the Kenyan setting.

Prolonged NG decompression >5 days is itself a cause of delayed GI recovery. The NG tube should be removed when output is <500 mL/day. Persistent high output (>1 L/day) suggests mechanical obstruction or an underlying complication -- do not simply replace the tube; investigate.

Early mobilization is the single most effective non-pharmacologic intervention. Patients who are out of bed on postoperative day 1 have a 40% shorter duration of ileus compared to those who remain in bed. In Kenyan surgical wards, mobilizing patients despite cultural expectations of 'bed rest after surgery' requires active nursing leadership and family education.

Thoracic epidural analgesia (TEA) at T6-12 with low-concentration bupivacaine (0.0625-0.125%) + fentanyl (2-5 mcg/mL) provides superior pain relief, reduces opioid consumption by 50-70%, and shortens the duration of POI by 20-30 hours compared to IV opioid PCA. When available, TEA is the gold standard for analgesia after open abdominal surgery. Epidural catheters can be maintained safely for 3-5 days postoperatively.

In the Kenyan setting, a simplified ERAS bundle adapted for resource limitations includes: (1) pre-operative carbohydrate loading (200 mL of clear apple juice or oral rehydration solution 2h before surgery), (2) opioid-sparing analgesia (paracetamol 1 g QDS + diclofenac 75 mg BD + tramadol 50-100 mg PRN), (3) early oral intake (sips of clear fluids 4h post-op, advancing as tolerated), (4) gum chewing TDS, (5) early mobilization (out of bed on day 1), and (6) NG tube removal by POD1. This bundle has been implemented at KNH with a 2-day reduction in hospital stay.

Hypokalemia (K+ <3.5 mmol/L) is present in 30-40% of prolonged ileus patients and is the single most easily correctable cause. Target K+ >4.0 mmol/L. Magnesium (target >0.8 mmol/L) and phosphate (target >0.8 mmol/L) are also important. Replete all three aggressively if deficient. Electrolyte depletion often requires >80 mmol K+ per day given via peripheral IV (max rate 10 mmol/hour).

When escalating treatment for refractory ileus: Step 1 -- correct electrolytes (K+, Mg++, PO4), minimize opioids. Step 2 -- add prokinetics (metoclopramide 10 mg TDS, erythromycin 200 mg TDS). Step 3 -- trial of neostigmine for colonic ileus/Ogilvie. Step 4 -- CT scan to exclude mechanical obstruction. Step 5 -- if CT negative, consider colonoscopic decompression. Step 6 -- if all measures fail after 7 days, diagnostic laparoscopy to identify occult adhesive bands or internal hernia.

operativeNuances.nuance: When performing adhesiolysis for adhesive small bowel obstruction after previous laparotomy, the safest entry technique is to enter the peritoneal cavity at a site remote from the previous incision. For a patient with a midline scar, enter through a left subcostal or left flank approach. Palpate the peritoneal cavity digitally before inserting any instrument. Divide adhesions sharply with scissors under direct vision -- do not use diathermy blindly, as thermal injury to underlying bowel can create a new perforation that mimics the pathology you are treating.

The most reliable intraoperative sign of bowel viability is arterial bleeding from a cut edge. If the serosa appears dusky or the bowel does not show peristalsis on mechanical stimulation, do not hesitate to resect back to healthy, bleeding tissue. Non-viable segments left in situ will perforate within 24-48 hours, converting a simple obstruction into a catastrophic peritonitis.

In Ogilvie syndrome (acute colonic pseudo-obstruction), neostigmine at 2.0-2.5 mg IV infused over 3-5 minutes achieves successful decompression in 80-90% of patients within 30 minutes. Monitor for bradycardia -- have atropine 0.6 mg at the bedside. Contraindicated if heart rate <60 bpm, active bronchospasm, or SBP <90 mmHg. Dilation of cecum >12 cm on X-ray with no clinical improvement after neostigmine is an indication for colonoscopic decompression or surgical cecostomy.

During laparotomy for adhesive small bowel obstruction, the most dangerous dissection zone is the 'frozen pelvis' -- the small bowel adherent in the pelvis after previous pelvic surgery. Rather than dissecting all loops free (which risks multiple enterotomies), identify the collapsed distal bowel and trace it proximally to find the transition point. If a single band is causing the obstruction, divide it and leave the remaining adhesions undisturbed ('minimal adhesiolysis' principle).

When a transition point is found in adhesive obstruction, assess the viability of the trapped bowel segment. Look for the 'three Ps': Pink color, Peristalsis (present spontaneously or on gentle stimulation), and Pulsatile mesenteric bleeding. If all three are present, simple adhesiolysis suffices. If the bowel is discolored but shows recovery after release, wrap with warm saline packs for 5-10 minutes and reassess. If still non-viable after 10 minutes, resect with primary anastomosis provided contamination is minimal.

prognosis.physiologicIleus_resolutionTime: Resolves in 2-4 days in 70% of patients; 90% by day 6
prognosis.prolongedIleus_incidence: Occurs in 10-15% of abdominal surgery patients; higher in open vs laparoscopic (OR 2.1)
prognosis.mortality: Ileus per se has negligible mortality; the underlying cause (obstruction, leak, abscess) carries the mortality risk
prognosis.hospitalStayProlongation: Increases mean hospital stay by 4-7 days; estimated economic cost per episode: KES 50,000-100,000 in Kenyan hospitals
prognosis.recurrenceRisk: Patients with prolonged ileus have higher risk of readmission (15-20%) and recurrent ileus in subsequent abdominal surgeries
prognosis.conversionToObstruction: Risk of progression to mechanical obstruction requiring surgery is 5-10% if ileus persists >7 days
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Opioid-sparing analgesia, early mobilization, electrolyte normalization, and early CT when ileus extends beyond day 4 are the key modifiable determinants
